name: Babel AST + Plugin Pipeline
summary: Babel transforms JavaScript via plugins, each operating on the ESTree-shaped AST. The IR is the AST itself, mutated in place by visitor functions; plugins register interest in specific node types and rewrite them. Optimised for transformation throughput, not optimisation depth.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Babel transforms JavaScript via plugins, each operating on the ESTree-shaped AST. The "IR" in Babel is the AST itself, mutated in place by visitor functions; plugins register interest in specific node types and rewrite them. The transformation pipeline composes plugins in order, but each plugin sees the AST as-is — there is no separate intermediate form.

sections.title: Transformation-throughput focus
sections.content:
The pragmatic consequence: Babel's representation is the same shape across all phases, which makes plugin authoring trivial and plugin composition near-free. The cost is that some optimizations want a different shape (SSA, sea-of-nodes, etc.) and Babel cannot offer those without breaking its plugin contract.

Babel optimizes for *transformation* throughput, not *optimization* depth — which fits its actual use case (transpiling, bundling, minifying) better than a heavyweight optimizer would. The Rust-based modern alternatives (swc, oxc, Rolldown) preserve this AST-centric model while implementing the visitor pipeline in a faster systems language.

sections.title: Visitor rewrite example
